title: The doer should not grade itself
summary: An honest evaluation separates the system doing the work from the system judging it, or the score measures agreement, not success.
body_html:
The first rule of honest evaluation is role separation. If the same model writes an answer and grades the answer, the score drifts toward what the model already likes: its own style, its own assumptions, its own mistakes. Self-grading rewards confidence and fluency, which are exactly the traits that fool a casual reader.

Separation means the grader is a different system with different blind spots, judging against a fixed standard set before the run. The doer produces, the rater judges, and the two do not share a stake in the outcome. This single move removes one of the largest sources of inflated AI evaluation results.

The same logic scales up. When you use a model to judge other models, vary the family and the prompt so the judge does not share the doer's failure modes. An evaluation where the judge and the judged think alike is closer to a mirror than a measurement.
key_points: Self-grading rewards style, confidence, and shared assumptions, not task success.
Separate the doer from the rater, judging against a standard fixed before the run.
The grader should have different blind spots than the doer.
A judge that thinks like the judged is a mirror, not a measurement.

links.title: Your AI Product Needs Evals
links.note: A practical guide to building real, separated evaluations.

links.title: Anthropic: Define Success and Build Evaluations
links.note: Setting measurable criteria before you run.

flashcards.front: Why should the doer not grade its own work?
flashcards.back: It rewards its own style, confidence, and assumptions instead of task success.

flashcards.front: What does role separation require?
flashcards.back: A different grader with different blind spots, judging a standard fixed before the run.

flashcards.front: What is a judge that thinks like the judged?
flashcards.back: A mirror, not a measurement.

quiz.q: What does a model that grades its own output tend to reward?
choices: Shorter answers, regardless of whether they are correct.
Strictly correct task success and nothing else at all.
Mostly random outcomes that do not track quality.
Its own style, confidence, and shared assumptions.
quiz.explain: Self-grading optimizes for what the model already likes, inflating the score.

quiz.q: When using a model as a judge, why vary its family and prompt from the doer's?
choices: So the judge does not share the doer's blind spots and failure modes.
So the evaluation burns more tokens overall and therefore looks more thorough to a reviewer.
Because a judge model must always be larger than the doer.
So the eval runs more slowly and gives the judge time to think.
quiz.explain: Decorrelating the judge from the doer is what keeps the score a measurement.

exercise.prompt: You use the same model and prompt to both write answers and grade them, and the scores look great. What is the risk?
choices: There is no real risk at all, since two perfectly matched models will grade each other fairly.
Self-grading inflates scores by rewarding the model's own style and shared blind spots.
The shared grader will be far too harsh and deflate every score.
It only affects how fast the evaluation runs, not the scores.
exercise.explain: A grader that shares the doer's assumptions measures agreement, not task success.

outcome: You can explain why self-grading inflates results and how role separation fixes it.

sources.claim: Eval results should be reported with uncertainty, not a single point number.
sources.title: Anthropic: A statistical approach to model evals

title: Catch rate, false alarms, and discrimination
summary: A detector's catch rate alone is meaningless; what matters is catch rate minus false alarm rate, the discrimination.
body_html:
Suppose you build a checker that flags bad model outputs. It catches 90% of the bad ones. That sounds great until you learn it also flags 85% of the good ones. A detector that flags almost everything has a high catch rate and no skill. Catch rate alone cannot tell a real detector from a trigger-happy one.

A useful single number is discrimination: the catch rate minus the false alarm rate (also called Youden's J). It asks how much more often the detector fires on real problems than on clean cases. A discrimination near zero means the detector is guessing; a high one means it genuinely separates good from bad. It is a quick summary at one threshold, not the whole story. When the base rate is skewed, or false positives and false negatives carry different costs, also look at precision, recall, and the full ROC curve. Always report both underlying rates, not just the catch.

This generalizes past detectors. Any time you measure a model finding, flagging, or classifying something, ask for the false positive side of the ledger. A metric that only counts hits will reward the loudest model, not the most accurate one.
key_points: Catch rate alone cannot distinguish skill from flagging everything.
Discrimination = catch rate minus false alarm rate (Youden's J).
Near zero means guessing; high means it truly separates good from bad.
It is a one-threshold summary; precision, recall, and ROC matter when costs or base rates are skewed.
Always report the false positive side, not just the hits.

links.title: Promptfoo
links.note: Run assertions and measure detector behavior across cases.

links.title: Your AI Product Needs Evals
links.note: Why balanced metrics beat a single headline number.

flashcards.front: Why is catch rate alone misleading?
flashcards.back: A detector that flags everything has a high catch rate and no real skill.

flashcards.front: What is discrimination?
flashcards.back: Catch rate minus false alarm rate: how much more it fires on real problems than clean cases.

flashcards.front: What does a discrimination near zero mean?
flashcards.back: The detector is essentially guessing.

quiz.q: A detector catches 95% of bad outputs but also flags 90% of good ones. What does this tell you?
choices: It is an excellent detector, since it manages to catch almost every single bad output.
Its discrimination is low (about 0.05), so it is close to flagging everything.
Its high catch rate alone proves the detector is working well.
False alarms do not matter as long as the catch rate is high.
quiz.explain: Discrimination of 0.95 minus 0.90 is 0.05, near guessing.

quiz.q: What must you always report alongside a detector's catch rate?
choices: The sampling temperature that was used during the detection run.
Only the total token count the detector consumed.
The false alarm rate, so discrimination can be computed.
The name of the underlying model doing the detecting.
quiz.explain: Without the false positive rate, the catch rate is uninterpretable.

exercise.prompt: This is a real confusion matrix. Change the counts and watch the catch rate, false alarm rate, and discrimination update live.
exercise.question: Adjust the counts so discrimination is at least 0.60, then check.
exercise.explain: Discrimination = catch rate (TP/(TP+FN)) minus false alarm rate (FP/(FP+TN)). Catching more real problems or flagging fewer clean cases raises it.

outcome: You can compute discrimination and explain why catch rate alone is not enough.

title: Pre-register the claim
summary: Decide what you are testing and what would count as success before you run, so the result cannot be reshaped to fit the data.
body_html:
The cheapest way to fool yourself is to run an experiment, look at the data, and then decide what the experiment was testing. Once you have seen the results, almost any pattern can be narrated into a finding. Pre-registration blocks this: you write down the exact claim, the metric, and the success threshold before you run, and you commit to reporting whatever comes back.

A good pre-registration is specific enough to be wrong. It names the hypothesis, how it will be measured, the sample, and the cutoff that separates a real effect from noise. If the result does not clear the threshold you set in advance, that is a null, and a null reported honestly is a real finding, not a failure.

This is the discipline that turns a demo into evidence. Without it, evaluation drifts toward whatever story the data happens to support. With it, you can trust your own conclusions, because you decided the rules before you could be tempted by the answer.
key_points: Write the exact claim, metric, and success threshold before running.
A good pre-registration is specific enough to be proven wrong.
A null result reported honestly is a real finding, not a failure.
Pre-registration is what turns a demo into trustworthy evidence.

links.title: Anthropic: Define Success and Build Evaluations
links.note: Designing measurable success criteria up front.

links.title: Your AI Product Needs Evals
links.note: Committing to criteria before iterating on results.

flashcards.front: What does pre-registration commit you to?
flashcards.back: The exact claim, metric, and threshold, plus reporting whatever result comes back.

flashcards.front: What makes a pre-registration good?
flashcards.back: It is specific enough to be proven wrong.

flashcards.front: How should you treat an honest null result?
flashcards.back: As a real finding, not a failure.

quiz.q: Why decide the success threshold before running the experiment?
choices: Because pre-registered success thresholds are required by data-protection regulation.
To avoid having to use any quantitative metric at all.
To deliberately slow the experiment down for review.
Because after seeing the data, almost any pattern can be narrated into a finding.
quiz.explain: Pre-committing the rules prevents post-hoc storytelling.

quiz.q: Your pre-registered effect does not clear its threshold. What is the honest move?
choices: Report the null; a null reported honestly is a real finding.
Quietly re-run it until it passes by chance one time.
Drop the experiment silently and never mention it again.
Lower the success threshold after the fact until the result finally passes.
quiz.explain: Honoring the pre-registered threshold is the whole point of pre-registration.

exercise.prompt: Put the steps of a pre-registered evaluation in order.
items: Write down the exact claim and the metric
Set the success threshold that separates a real effect from noise
Report whatever result comes back, even a null
exercise.explain: Decide the claim, metric, and threshold before running, then report honestly. Choosing any of these after seeing the data invites cherry-picking.

outcome: You can pre-register a claim with a metric and threshold and honor a null result.

title: Decorrelation versus competence
summary: Two judges that agree only help if they fail independently; shared blind spots and a capability ceiling both quietly cap what an eval can measure.
body_html:
Using multiple judges is only useful if they are decorrelated, meaning they make different mistakes. Two judges from the same model family with the same prompt will tend to agree even when they are both wrong, so their agreement is not extra evidence. Real robustness comes from judges that fail in different directions, so that one catches what another misses.

Competence sets a second, subtler ceiling. A judge can only catch an error it is itself capable of recognizing. If a task is at or beyond the frontier of what current models can do, even a panel of strong judges may all share the same gap, and their consensus will look confident while missing the real answer. Agreement among the limited is not the same as correctness.

The two ideas combine into a practical caution. When an evaluation depends on models judging other models, ask both whether the judges are decorrelated and whether they are competent at the thing being judged. If a result is only verifiable by giving the judges decorrelated context they would not otherwise have, that requirement is part of the finding, not a footnote.
key_points: Multiple judges help only if they fail independently, not if they merely agree.
Same-family, same-prompt judges share blind spots, so their agreement is weak evidence.
A judge can only catch errors it is itself competent to recognize.
Ask both: are the judges decorrelated, and are they competent at this task?

links.title: Stanford HELM
links.note: A multi-metric benchmark that resists single-judge blind spots.

links.title: OpenAI Evals
links.note: Framework for building benchmarks across diverse cases.

flashcards.front: When do multiple judges actually help?
flashcards.back: Only when they are decorrelated and fail in different directions.

flashcards.front: Why is same-family judge agreement weak evidence?
flashcards.back: They share blind spots, so they agree even when both are wrong.

flashcards.front: What is the competence ceiling?
flashcards.back: A judge can only catch an error it is itself capable of recognizing.

quiz.q: Two judges from the same model with the same prompt agree. How strong is that evidence?
choices: Very strong evidence, because two separate judges both independently agreed on it.
Weak, because they share blind spots and can be wrong together.
Conclusive proof, since agreement settles the question.
Irrelevant to the evaluation, since judges never matter.
quiz.explain: Correlated judges agreeing adds little; they can fail in the same direction.

quiz.q: Why can a panel of strong judges still miss the answer on a frontier task?
choices: Frontier tasks are always easy enough that any strong judge can solve them without much trouble.
The judges were simply too slow to reach the answer.
They may all share the same capability gap, so consensus looks confident but is wrong.
The panel used too few tokens to judge the task well.
quiz.explain: Competence caps what any judge can recognize; shared gaps survive consensus.

exercise.prompt: Three judges from the same model family, with the same prompt, all agree the answer is correct. How much extra confidence does their agreement give?
choices: A lot of extra confidence, since three separate judges all independently agreed on it.
Little; correlated judges share blind spots, so they can be wrong together.
It is conclusive proof that the answer is correct.
None at all, since these models cannot judge anything.
exercise.explain: Agreement only adds evidence when judges fail independently; same-family judges do not.

outcome: You can reason about judge decorrelation and the competence ceiling of an evaluation.

title: Confounds: when the comparison lies
summary: A confound is a hidden difference that explains your result instead of the cause you claimed; finding it is often the whole job.
body_html:
A confound is a variable that differs alongside the one you care about, so your result could be caused by either. If model A looks more honest than model B but A was also tested on easier questions, difficulty is a confound: the honesty gap might be entirely about the questions. The conclusion is not wrong because the numbers are wrong; it is wrong because the comparison was not clean.

Confounds are the default failure of AI evaluation because the systems are complex and many things move at once. Prompt wording, task difficulty, answer length, ordering, and which examples you happened to pick can each create a difference that masquerades as the effect you wanted. The discipline is to hold everything else constant and change only the one thing under test.

The hardest confounds are the ones built into the design. A study can be internally consistent and still measure the wrong thing because two causes were tangled from the start, such as social pressure mixed with genuine new evidence. The reliable defense is adversarial: before trusting a result, actively look for the alternative explanation that would make it false. If you cannot rule it out, it belongs in the writeup.
key_points: A confound is a hidden difference that could explain the result instead of your claimed cause.
Clean comparisons hold everything constant and change only the variable under test.
Prompt, difficulty, length, ordering, and example choice are common confounds.
Before trusting a result, hunt for the alternative explanation; if you cannot rule it out, report it.

links.title: An Introduction to Statistical Learning
links.note: Background on confounding and controlled comparison.

links.title: Stanford HELM
links.note: Holding conditions constant across many models and tasks.

flashcards.front: What is a confound?
flashcards.back: A hidden difference that could explain your result instead of the cause you claimed.

flashcards.front: What is a clean comparison?
flashcards.back: One that holds everything constant and changes only the variable under test.

flashcards.front: What is the reliable defense against confounds?
flashcards.back: Adversarially hunt the alternative explanation; if you cannot rule it out, report it.

quiz.q: Model A scores as 'more honest' than B, but A was tested on easier questions. What is the flaw?
choices: Honesty is something that simply cannot be measured in any reliable way at all.
The metric used was discrimination instead of accuracy.
The sample of questions was simply far too large here.
Difficulty is a confound; the gap may be about the questions, not honesty.
quiz.explain: An uncontrolled difference in difficulty can fully explain the result.

quiz.q: What is the best habit for catching design-level confounds?
choices: Adversarially search for the alternative explanation before believing the conclusion.
Run the whole experiment only once and trust the result.
Use a single judge model throughout so that the scores always stay perfectly consistent.
Trust any result that comes out looking clean and tidy.
quiz.explain: Actively trying to break your own result surfaces tangled causes.

exercise.prompt: An eval concludes model A 'follows instructions better' than B. You then learn A's prompts were all short and B's were all long and complex. What should you conclude?
choices: Prompt complexity is a confound; the comparison cannot support the claim.
B is better, since its prompts were longer and harder.
The result is solid and needs no further checking of any kind before you believe it.
A is clearly better at following instructions overall.
exercise.explain: Because prompt complexity differed with the model, it could explain the gap on its own.

outcome: You can spot confounds and demand a clean comparison before trusting a result.

title: The noise floor and confidence intervals
summary: Small evals are noisy; without an uncertainty estimate, a difference can be pure chance, so report intervals, not point scores.
body_html:
Run the same eval twice on a stochastic model and you will get slightly different numbers. That spread is the noise floor: the size of differences you would see even when nothing real changed. A measured gap smaller than the noise floor is not a finding, it is the measurement breathing. The first question about any score difference is whether it clears the noise.

The bootstrap is a simple, assumption-light way to estimate that noise. You resample your results many times with replacement, recompute the metric each time, and look at the spread of those recomputed values. The middle range of that spread is a confidence interval: a plausible range for the true value (strictly, an interval whose construction would contain the true value a set fraction of the time across repeated samples). When you compare two models on the same test items, do not just check whether their separate intervals overlap, which is a conservative test that throws away the pairing. Instead bootstrap the per-item difference between the two models and ask whether that difference's interval excludes zero. Paired comparison is more sensitive because it cancels the shared difficulty of each item.

Sample size drives all of this. Five examples can produce a dramatic-looking gap that vanishes at fifty. Report the interval alongside the number, state the sample size, and treat a wide interval as a signal to gather more data rather than a result to announce.
key_points: The noise floor is the variation you see when nothing real changed.
A gap smaller than the noise floor is not a finding.
Bootstrap resampling estimates a confidence interval with few assumptions.
To compare two models on the same items, bootstrap the paired per-item difference and check whether its interval excludes zero, rather than eyeballing separate-interval overlap.
Report intervals and sample size; a wide interval means gather more data.

links.title: An Introduction to Statistical Learning
links.note: Resampling, the bootstrap, and uncertainty.

links.title: DeepLearning.AI: Evaluating and Debugging Generative AI
links.note: Tracking and quantifying variation in generative evals.

flashcards.front: What is the noise floor?
flashcards.back: The size of differences you would see even when nothing real changed.

flashcards.front: What does the bootstrap give you?
flashcards.back: A confidence interval for the metric, by resampling results with replacement.

flashcards.front: How should you compare two models on the same test set?
flashcards.back: Bootstrap the per-item difference and check whether its interval excludes zero; comparing separate intervals for overlap is too conservative and ignores the pairing.

quiz.q: Model A scores 72% and B scores 70% on the same 20 test items, and their separate intervals overlap. What is the right move?
choices: Conclude A is better, since 72% clearly beats 70%.
Bootstrap the per-item difference (A minus B) and check whether its interval excludes zero.
Conclude there is definitely no difference, since the intervals overlap.
Raise the sampling temperature and run the whole thing again.
quiz.explain: Overlapping separate intervals are inconclusive and conservative; the paired difference is the sensitive, correct test.

quiz.q: Why use a bootstrap confidence interval?
choices: To help you choose the right sampling temperature for the whole evaluation run.
To make the reported score come out higher.
To estimate a plausible range for the true value with few assumptions.
To remove the need for collecting any test data.
quiz.explain: Resampling quantifies how much the score could vary by chance.

exercise.prompt: You are comparing two models on the SAME 50 test items. How should you test whether A really beats B?
choices: Pick whichever model happens to have the higher raw score.
Run each model once on the items and compare the totals.
Check whether their two separate confidence intervals overlap.
Bootstrap the per-item difference (A minus B) and check whether its interval excludes zero.
exercise.explain: Paired data calls for bootstrapping the difference; comparing separate intervals is conservative and ignores the pairing.

outcome: You can judge whether a score difference clears the noise floor using intervals.

title: Sycophancy and capitulation
summary: Models can abandon a correct answer under social pressure; measuring this cleanly is hard because deference and genuine updating look alike.
body_html:
Sycophancy is a model telling you what you want to hear. Capitulation is a sharp form of it: the model gives a correct answer, you push back, and it switches to a wrong one to agree with you. Both matter because they make a model less reliable exactly when a confident user is mistaken, which is when reliability matters most.

Measuring capitulation cleanly is harder than it looks, and the difficulty is instructive. When a model changes its answer after pushback, two very different things can be happening: it is genuinely updating on a good argument, or it is deferring to social pressure. From the outside they look identical, so a naive test conflates rational updating with capitulation and overstates the problem.

The honest approaches separate the two. You can give pressure that carries no real evidence, so any change is deference rather than updating. You can probe belief directly rather than only watching the final answer flip. And you have to account for capability: a model that is simply right and certain rarely capitulates on a task it has mastered, so a null result can mean a clean model or just an easy task. Naming which one you measured is part of the result.
key_points: Sycophancy is telling the user what they want; capitulation is dropping a correct answer under pushback.
An answer flip can be rational updating or social deference; from outside they look the same.
Clean tests use evidence-free pressure or probe belief directly, not just the final flip.
Capability confounds it: a mastered task yields few capitulations, so name what the null means.

links.title: Anthropic: Define Success and Build Evaluations
links.note: Designing criteria that isolate the behavior you mean.

links.title: Stanford HELM
links.note: Benchmarks spanning behaviors beyond raw accuracy.

flashcards.front: What is capitulation?
flashcards.back: A model giving a correct answer, then switching to a wrong one after the user pushes back.

flashcards.front: Why is capitulation hard to measure?
flashcards.back: An answer flip can be rational updating or social deference, and they look identical.

flashcards.front: How does capability confound capitulation tests?
flashcards.back: A model certain and right on a mastered task rarely capitulates, so a null can just mean an easy task.

quiz.q: A model changes its answer after a user objects. Why is that not automatically capitulation?
choices: Because the user who objects is essentially always right about the underlying question anyway.
Because capitulation is not a real phenomenon at all.
Because models are not capable of changing their answers.
Because the change could be rational updating on a good argument, not social deference.
quiz.explain: Updating and deference look the same from outside, so the flip alone is ambiguous.

quiz.q: How do you make pushback test deference rather than reasoning?
choices: Give pushback that carries no real evidence, so any change is deference.
Only ever test it on very easy questions.
Let the model grade its own responses.
Give the model pushback that carries strong, genuinely new evidence each time.
quiz.explain: Evidence-free pressure isolates social deference from rational updating.

exercise.prompt: You test capitulation by pushing back on puzzles the model already solves at near 100% accuracy, and see almost no answer changes. What does this null most likely mean?
choices: The model never actually had a correct answer to begin with.
The model is provably immune to social pressure everywhere.
The task was too easy to detect capitulation; a clean model and an easy task look the same here.
Capitulation is fundamentally impossible to study at all.
exercise.explain: On a mastered task, certainty suppresses capitulation, so the null does not isolate a clean model from an easy test.

outcome: You can design a capitulation test that separates deference from updating and read its nulls honestly.

sources.claim: RLHF-trained assistants show measurable sycophancy, agreeing with users over being correct.
sources.title: Anthropic: Towards Understanding Sycophancy in Language Models

title: The not-measured ledger
summary: An honest evaluation states what it did not measure as plainly as what it did, because the gaps are where false confidence hides.
body_html:
Every evaluation has a boundary: things it could not observe, conditions it did not test, inputs it never saw. The not-measured ledger is the explicit list of those gaps, kept beside the results. It is the opposite of the usual instinct, which is to present what you measured and quietly let readers assume the rest was fine. The unstated gaps are exactly where overconfidence lives.

Some gaps are unavoidable. Real production inputs, rare edge cases, long-horizon behavior, and adversarial misuse are often impossible to fully cover before shipping. Naming them does not weaken the result; it tells an honest reader where the result applies and where it does not. A finding with a clear boundary is more trustworthy than one that pretends to have none.

The ledger also drives the next move. Each unmeasured item is verification debt: a question you owe an answer to, ideally with a scheduled human checkpoint where a person observes what the automated eval could not. The mature stance is not a perfect evaluation, which does not exist, but a complete and honest account of where the current one stops.
key_points: The not-measured ledger lists, beside the results, what the eval did not observe.
Unstated gaps are where false confidence hides.
Naming a boundary makes a result more trustworthy, not less.
Each gap is verification debt; schedule a human checkpoint to pay it down.

links.title: Anthropic: Define Success and Build Evaluations
links.note: Scoping what an evaluation does and does not cover.

links.title: Your AI Product Needs Evals
links.note: Treating evaluation as an ongoing, honest practice.

flashcards.front: What is the not-measured ledger?
flashcards.back: An explicit list of what the evaluation did not observe, kept beside the results.

flashcards.front: Why state what you did not measure?
flashcards.back: Unstated gaps are where overconfidence hides; a clear boundary builds trust.

flashcards.front: What is verification debt?
flashcards.back: Each unmeasured item is a question you owe, ideally paid with a scheduled human checkpoint.

quiz.q: Why does naming the boundaries of an eval increase trust?
choices: It makes the whole evaluation look much weaker than it really is to a casual reader.
It tells an honest reader where the result applies and where it does not.
It quietly hides the gaps from a casual reader.
It removes the need to collect any real data.
quiz.explain: A finding with a clear boundary is more trustworthy than one pretending to have none.

quiz.q: What is the mature stance on evaluation completeness?
choices: Trust automated evaluations fully and skip human checks.
Insist on a perfect, complete evaluation before shipping.
Give a complete, honest account of where the current evaluation stops, and schedule checks for the gaps.
Measure essentially nothing and just ship the system.
quiz.explain: Perfect evals do not exist; honest boundaries plus scheduled human checks do.

exercise.prompt: Your evaluation never tested adversarial inputs. What should the report do?
choices: Say nothing about it and just let readers assume that part of the system was fine.
List it in the not-measured ledger and schedule a human check.
Claim the model is robust to them anyway, to be safe.
Delete the result so no one asks about the gap.
exercise.explain: Naming the gap and scheduling a check is more trustworthy than an unstated boundary.

outcome: You can keep a not-measured ledger and turn its gaps into scheduled verification.

title: Evaluation in production
summary: Offline evals only cover known cases; production needs tracing, monitoring, and a feedback loop to catch drift and real failures.
body_html:
An offline eval on a fixed test set tells you how the system does on cases you thought of. Production is different: inputs drift, users do unexpected things, and the costly failures are often the ones you did not anticipate. A system that scored well offline can still disappoint after launch, and without instrumentation you will not know why.

Observability is the fix. Trace each request end to end: the input, the retrieved context, the tool calls, and the final output. Log outcomes and monitor metrics over time so you can see distribution shift, regressions after a prompt or model change, and rare high-cost errors. The point is to make production behavior visible rather than inferred.

Then close the loop. Sample real traffic, label it, and feed it back into your eval set so the offline suite tracks reality. Online signals such as task success, user corrections, or thumbs complement offline scores. Evaluation is not a one-time gate before launch; it is a standing practice that production data continually feeds.
key_points: Offline evals cover anticipated cases; production has drift and surprises.
Trace each request (input, context, tool calls, output) and monitor over time.
Watch for distribution shift, post-change regressions, and rare high-cost failures.
Close the loop: sample and label production traffic back into the eval set.

links.title: Your AI Product Needs Evals
links.note: Treats evaluation as a standing, production-fed practice.

links.title: Anthropic: Define Success and Build Evaluations
links.note: Designing criteria you can monitor in production.

flashcards.front: Why are offline evals not enough?
flashcards.back: They only cover anticipated cases; production has drift and unexpected inputs.

flashcards.front: What does observability capture for an LLM system?
flashcards.back: End-to-end traces: input, retrieved context, tool calls, and output, monitored over time.

flashcards.front: How do you close the evaluation loop?
flashcards.back: Sample and label production traffic and feed it back into the offline eval set.

quiz.q: A system scores 95% offline but users complain after launch. Most likely cause?
choices: The model quietly got smaller after it was deployed.
The users are simply wrong about their own experience.
The offline score must have been faked or mis-recorded during the original testing.
Distribution shift: production inputs differ from the eval set.
quiz.explain: Real inputs often differ from a fixed test set; trace and sample production to find out how.

quiz.q: What turns evaluation into a standing practice rather than a launch gate?
choices: Feeding sampled, labeled production traffic back into the eval set.
Switching permanently to a larger and more capable model for the whole system.
Raising the sampling temperature on every eval run.
Running the evaluation once, just before the release.
quiz.explain: A feedback loop keeps the offline suite tracking real-world behavior.

exercise.prompt: After a prompt change, offline scores are unchanged but production error reports spike. What is the most useful next step?
choices: Trace failing production requests and add those cases to the eval set.
Raise the sampling temperature and rerun the offline eval.
Roll the change forward and hope the reports settle down.
Ignore the spike entirely, since the offline scores did not change at all afterward.
exercise.explain: Production traces reveal failures the fixed offline set missed; capture them to make the suite reflect reality.

outcome: You can explain why production evaluation needs tracing, monitoring, and a feedback loop.

title: LLM as a judge
summary: Using a model to grade outputs scales evaluation, but only with a clear rubric and an awareness of the judge's biases.
body_html:
When outputs are open-ended, there is no exact-match key, so teams increasingly use a strong model as the grader. It scales and correlates reasonably with human judgment, but it is only as good as the rubric you give it. A vague instruction like rate this 1 to 10 produces noisy, drifting scores. A good judge prompt states explicit criteria, defines what each score means, and ideally shows a few anchored examples, so the judgment is reproducible rather than a vibe.

LLM judges have characteristic biases you must design around. They tend to prefer longer answers, to favor a particular position in a pairwise comparison (order bias, whose direction depends on the model and prompt), and to rate their own family's outputs higher. Pairwise comparison (which of these two is better) is often more reliable than absolute scoring, and randomizing order plus swapping positions controls the position bias. None of this removes the need for the discipline from earlier in this track: the judge must stay decorrelated from the doer, and you must validate the judge against human labels on a sample.

The honest framing is that an LLM judge is a useful, scalable proxy, not ground truth. Treat its scores like any other metric: check them against humans, watch for the known biases, and report the rubric you used. A judge you have not validated is an opinion, not a measurement.
key_points: A clear rubric with defined score levels and anchors beats 'rate 1 to 10'.
Judges have biases: verbosity, position (first option), and self-preference.
Pairwise comparison with randomized, swapped order is more reliable than absolute scoring.
Validate the judge against human labels; an unvalidated judge is an opinion, not a measurement.

links.title: Judging LLM-as-a-Judge (MT-Bench, Chatbot Arena)
links.note: The paper that studied LLM judges, their agreement with humans, and their biases.

links.title: Your AI Product Needs Evals
links.note: Practical guidance on building and validating judge-based evals.

flashcards.front: What makes an LLM-judge prompt reproducible?
flashcards.back: Explicit criteria, defined score levels, and a few anchored examples, not 'rate 1 to 10'.

flashcards.front: Name three biases of LLM judges.
flashcards.back: Verbosity preference, order bias (favoring a fixed position), and self-preference.

flashcards.front: Why validate an LLM judge against humans?
flashcards.back: An unvalidated judge is an opinion, not a measurement; validation shows it tracks ground truth.

quiz.q: Your LLM judge gives noisy, inconsistent scores. What is the most likely fix?
choices: Switch to a bigger judge model and nothing else.
Give it an explicit rubric with defined score levels and anchored examples.
Raise the sampling temperature on the judge model.
Average together a great many more of the noisy random scores until they smooth out.
quiz.explain: A clear, anchored rubric is what turns a vibe into a reproducible judgment.

quiz.q: Why is pairwise comparison often preferred over absolute 1 to 10 scoring?
choices: It removes essentially all bias from the scoring.
It is noticeably faster to run than absolute one-to-ten scoring is in practice.
It is usually more reliable, and swapping order controls position bias.
It needs no rubric, since you only compare two answers.
quiz.explain: Relative judgments are more stable, and randomized/swapped order counters position bias.

exercise.goal: Turn a vague judge prompt into a reproducible, rubric-anchored one.
choices: Tell me whether the answer is basically good or bad.
Rate this answer from 1 to 10 and try to be fair.

Score the answer on three criteria, each 0 to 2: factual correctness, completeness, and clarity. Define each level (0 wrong/absent, 1 partial, 2 fully met), give the total, and quote the evidence for each score.
exercise.explain: A reproducible judge prompt names explicit criteria, defines each score level, and asks for evidence.

outcome: You can design and validate an LLM-as-judge eval and control its known biases.

title: When raters disagree
summary: An eval is only as trustworthy as its labels; measure rater agreement, and correct for the agreement you would get by chance.
body_html:
Every eval rests on labels, and labels come from raters who disagree. If two people (or two model judges) cannot consistently apply your rubric, the gold standard you are scoring against is shaky, and no amount of model improvement will show up cleanly. So before trusting a benchmark, measure inter-rater reliability: how often do independent raters give the same label?

Raw agreement is tempting but misleading when one answer is common. If 85% of cases are yes, two raters who each say yes about 85% of the time will agree roughly 75% of the time just by chance (0.85 squared plus 0.15 squared), so 90% raw agreement is only modestly above that chance baseline. Chance-corrected measures like Cohen's kappa account for this: they ask how much agreement exceeds what you would expect at random. A high kappa means the rubric is genuinely shared; a low one means it needs sharpening.

The practical loop is to pilot the rubric on a sample, measure agreement, and when raters diverge, adjudicate and tighten the definitions until they converge. Disagreement is not noise to average away; it is a signal that the task is underspecified. Fix the rubric, then collect the labels you will actually trust.
key_points: An eval is only as good as the agreement among the raters who made its labels.
Raw agreement overstates reliability when one label is common.
Chance-corrected measures like Cohen's kappa show agreement beyond random.
Rater disagreement signals an underspecified rubric; adjudicate and tighten it.

links.title: Cohen's kappa
links.note: The standard chance-corrected agreement statistic, with worked examples.

links.title: Your AI Product Needs Evals
links.note: Why label quality and rater agreement underpin every eval.

flashcards.front: Why measure inter-rater reliability before trusting a benchmark?
flashcards.back: The eval is only as good as its labels; if raters cannot agree, the gold standard is shaky.

flashcards.front: Why is raw agreement misleading?
flashcards.back: When one label is common, raters agree a lot by chance, so high raw agreement can mean little.

flashcards.front: What does Cohen's kappa add over raw agreement?
flashcards.back: It corrects for chance, showing how much agreement exceeds random.

quiz.q: Two raters agree 90% of the time, but 'pass' is the label 85% of the time. How impressed should you be?
choices: It proves the rubric is essentially perfect as written.
Raw agreement between the two raters on its own is always entirely sufficient to trust the rubric.
Very impressed, since 90% agreement is genuinely high.
Not very; chance agreement is already high, so use a chance-corrected measure like kappa.
quiz.explain: With a skewed label, 90% raw agreement is barely above chance; kappa reveals the real reliability.

quiz.q: Your raters keep disagreeing on a label. What does this most likely indicate?
choices: The rubric is underspecified and needs tightening.
You simply need to add more raters to the pool.
Inter-rater agreement does not really matter for this kind of label.
The model being evaluated is just bad at the task.
quiz.explain: Persistent disagreement is a signal that the task definition is unclear, not just noise.

exercise.prompt: On a task where 95% of items are 'safe', two judges agree 94% of the time. What is the honest read?
choices: Excellent reliability, since 94% agreement is very high.
Weak evidence; with a 95%-skewed label, chance agreement is ~90%, so correct for chance (kappa).
The two judges are clearly broken and disagree at random.
Raw agreement of 94% settles the question on its own.
exercise.explain: A heavily skewed label inflates raw agreement; chance-corrected kappa is needed to judge real reliability.

outcome: You can assess label quality with chance-corrected rater agreement and fix an underspecified rubric.

title: Precision, recall, F1, and the ROC curve
summary: One number rarely tells the whole story. The metric family you pick encodes what kind of mistake you can least afford.
body_html:
Accuracy collapses a whole confusion matrix into one number, and on imbalanced data it lies: a detector that never fires scores 99% accuracy when only 1% of cases are positive. The precision and recall pair keeps the two error types separate. Precision asks, of everything you flagged, how much was real (it punishes false alarms). Recall, the catch rate from the earlier lesson, asks, of everything that was real, how much you caught (it punishes misses). You almost always trade one against the other.

F1 is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, a single score that stays low unless both are decent. It is a reasonable default when false alarms and misses cost about the same, but it hides which one is failing, so report the pair alongside it. When the two costs differ, weight them: a cancer screen tolerates false alarms to avoid misses, a spam filter does the opposite.

The ROC curve steps back from any single threshold. It plots the true positive rate against the false positive rate as you sweep the decision threshold from strict to lenient, and the area under it (AUC) summarizes how well the model's scores rank a random positive above a random negative, independent of where you set the cutoff. AUC is useful for comparing rankers, but it can flatter a model on imbalanced data; the precision-recall curve is the more honest companion there. Pick the threshold from the curve to match your real costs, not the default 0.5.
key_points: Accuracy misleads on imbalanced data; keep precision and recall separate.
Precision punishes false alarms; recall (catch rate) punishes misses; they trade off.
F1 is the harmonic mean of the two and stays low unless both are decent.
ROC/AUC measure ranking across all thresholds; PR curves are more honest when positives are rare.

links.title: scikit-learn: Metrics and scoring
links.note: The reference for precision, recall, F1, ROC, and PR in practice.

links.title: Google ML Crash Course: ROC and AUC
links.note: A short, visual treatment of the ROC curve and AUC.

flashcards.front: Precision vs recall in one line each.
flashcards.back: Precision: of what you flagged, how much was real (punishes false alarms). Recall: of what was real, how much you caught (punishes misses).

flashcards.front: What does AUC actually measure?
flashcards.back: The probability that the model scores a random positive above a random negative: ranking quality, independent of the threshold.

flashcards.front: When is F1 the wrong default?
flashcards.back: When a false alarm and a miss have very different costs. Then weight precision and recall to match those costs instead.

quiz.q: On a dataset with 1% positives, a model predicts negative for everything. Its accuracy is:
choices: Undefined, since one class is essentially absent from the data entirely.
About 99%, which is why accuracy alone is misleading here
About 1%, matching the share of positives.
Exactly 50%, since it always guesses negative.
quiz.explain: It is right on all 99% of negatives, so accuracy is ~99% despite zero recall. Accuracy hides the failure on imbalanced data.

quiz.q: You raise the decision threshold so the model fires less often. What typically happens?
choices: Recall rises while precision falls off sharply.
Both precision and recall rise at the same time.
Precision tends to rise while recall falls
Nothing changes; the threshold is purely cosmetic.
quiz.explain: Firing less often means fewer false alarms (higher precision) but more misses (lower recall). That is the core tradeoff.

quiz.q: Why prefer a precision-recall curve over ROC when positives are rare?
choices: PR curves simply ignore false negatives entirely.
PR curves need no labels to be computed at all.
ROC analysis is always the wrong tool to reach for.
A large true-negative pool keeps the false positive rate small, so ROC/AUC can look strong even when precision is poor
quiz.explain: With many negatives, FPR stays low and AUC looks good even when most positive predictions are wrong. PR exposes that directly.

exercise.prompt: Each dot is a scored example: blue label 1 is a true positive case, label 0 is a true negative. Drag the threshold and watch the operating point move along the ROC curve.
exercise.question: Find a threshold that keeps recall at 1.0 (catch every positive). What does it cost you in precision?
exercise.explain: Lowering the threshold catches more positives (higher recall) but admits more false positives (lower precision). The ROC curve traces every such tradeoff; you choose the point that matches your costs.

outcome: Choose and defend a metric family, and read an operating point off an ROC curve instead of trusting accuracy.

sources.claim: AUC equals the probability a random positive is scored above a random negative.
sources.title: Receiver operating characteristic (overview of AUC)

title: Significance, p-values, and the multiple-comparisons trap
summary: A score difference is not a result until you know it survives noise. P-values and resampling tell you whether to believe it, and running many tests quietly inflates false positives.
body_html:
You run model B against model A and it wins by two points. Real, or noise? A significance test answers a narrow question: if there were truly no difference, how often would chance alone produce a gap this large or larger? That tail probability is the p-value. A small p-value means the observed gap would be surprising under no real difference, so you lean toward a real effect. It does not tell you the effect is large, or important, or that the opposite hypothesis is false, three things p-values are routinely misread as saying.

For eval scores, the cleanest tool is often the bootstrap from the noise-floor lesson: resample your test items with replacement many times, recompute the metric each time, and read the spread. A 95% interval that excludes zero difference is the practical analogue of significance, and it shows the magnitude, not just a yes or no. Crucially, the interval narrows with more eval items (more n), not with more resamples; resampling only sharpens the estimate of an interval whose width is set by your sample size.

The quiet killer is multiple comparisons. Test twenty prompt variants at the 0.05 level and, even if none truly differs, you expect about one to look significant by chance. Leaderboards, ablations, and hyperparameter sweeps are all multiple comparisons in disguise. Pre-register the one comparison you care about, or correct for the number of tests (a Bonferroni-style or false-discovery-rate adjustment), or hold out a fresh confirmation set. Otherwise you are mining noise and calling it a finding.
key_points: A p-value is the chance of a gap this large under no real difference; it is not the effect size or the probability the result is true.
A bootstrap 95% interval that excludes zero is the practical, magnitude-aware version of significance.
Interval width is set by the number of eval items (n), not the number of resamples.
Running many comparisons inflates false positives; pre-register, correct, or confirm on held-out data.

links.title: Wikipedia: Multiple comparisons problem
links.note: Why testing many hypotheses inflates false positives, and how corrections work.

links.title: Wikipedia: Bootstrapping (statistics)
links.note: The resampling method behind the confidence interval in the lab.

flashcards.front: State what a p-value is, precisely.
flashcards.back: The probability of observing a difference at least this large if there were truly no difference. Not the effect size, not the probability the result is true.

flashcards.front: Does running more bootstrap resamples narrow the confidence interval?
flashcards.back: No. More resamples stabilize the estimate of the interval. The width shrinks only with more eval items (larger n).

flashcards.front: Why are leaderboards a multiple-comparisons hazard?
flashcards.back: Comparing many models or variants means many chances for noise to produce an apparent winner. Some 'significant' gaps are expected by chance.

quiz.q: A result has p = 0.03. Which reading is correct?
choices: If there were no real difference, a gap this large would occur about 3% of the time
It means the measured effect is therefore guaranteed to be a large and practically important one.
It means the null hypothesis has been proven false.
There is a 97% chance that the effect is genuinely real.
quiz.explain: The p-value is a tail probability under the null. It says nothing directly about effect size or the probability that the hypothesis is true.

quiz.q: Your bootstrap 95% interval for B minus A is [-0.5, +3.1] points. The honest conclusion is:
choices: B is significantly worse, since the interval dips below zero.
The interval includes zero, so the data do not establish a real difference
Run more resamples until the interval finally excludes zero.
B is significantly better, since most of the interval sits above zero anyway.
quiz.explain: Spanning zero means no clear difference at this sample size. Cranking resamples will not move that; you need more eval items.

quiz.q: You test 20 variants at the 0.05 level and one looks significant. Before believing it, you should:
choices: Lower the significance threshold all the way to 0.5.
Publish the significant variant right away.
Expect roughly one false positive by chance and confirm on fresh held-out data or apply a correction
Test twenty more variants and see what turns up.
quiz.explain: At 0.05 across 20 tests you expect about one spurious hit. Correct for the count or confirm on a held-out set.

exercise.prompt: These are per-item pass/fail results from one eval run (1 = passed). Resample them to see the distribution of the mean accuracy and its 95% interval. Then raise n and watch the interval tighten.
exercise.question: Set n to 24, then to 120. What happens to the width of the 95% interval, and why?
exercise.explain: More eval items shrink the interval because the mean is estimated from more evidence. More resamples only smooth the estimate of that interval; they do not narrow it.

outcome: Decide whether a score difference is real, report it as an interval, and avoid the multiple-comparisons trap.

title: Capstone: design an honest eval
summary: Put the whole track to work. Given a capability to measure, choose the metric, the baseline, the role separation, and name the failure you would most likely miss.
body_html:
This capstone walks one realistic brief end to end. Your team ships an agent that drafts replies to customer support tickets, and leadership wants a number for how good it is before a wider rollout. A weak eval would average a 1-to-5 quality rating from the same model that wrote the replies and call it 4.3 out of 5. Your job is to design something you would actually trust, using the discipline from this track: separate the doer from the grader, pre-register the claim, pick a metric tied to the real cost, set an honest baseline, and keep a ledger of what you did not measure.

Work each step in order. There is one defensible answer per step, with reasoning shown after you choose. The point is not trivia; it is the sequence of decisions that turns a vibe into a measurement.
key_points: Separation of roles: the system under test cannot also be its own grader.
Tie the metric to the real cost, but only measure what is observable: outcomes of never-sent drafts are counterfactual and need a pilot or A/B test.
A result needs a baseline and a pre-registered claim to mean anything.
Name the failure mode your eval cannot see, and log it as verification debt.

links.title: Anthropic: A statistical approach to model evals
links.note: How to report eval results with uncertainty rather than a single number.

flashcards.front: First question to ask of any eval result.
flashcards.back: Who graded it, and were they independent of who produced it? If the doer is the grader, the number is suspect.

flashcards.front: What turns a score into a claim?
flashcards.back: A pre-registered hypothesis, a baseline to beat, and an interval that accounts for noise.

quiz.q: The single biggest threat to the support-agent eval described here is:
choices: Reporting a confidence interval with the result.
Choosing a properly held-out test set up front.
Using a few too many test tickets in the set.
Letting the agent's own model grade its replies
quiz.explain: Self-grading is the classic role-separation failure: the grader shares the doer's blind spots and biases.

quiz.q: A good baseline for the support agent would be:
choices: The current human-written replies (or the prior template system) on the same tickets
A second independent run of the very same agent on exactly the same set of support tickets.
The agent's own self-reported confidence score.
No baseline at all; just report the raw score.
quiz.explain: A baseline is what you must beat to matter: the status quo the agent would replace, scored on the same items.

exercise.prompt: Design the eval for the support-reply agent, one decision at a time.
exercise.scenario_html: Brief: an agent drafts replies to customer support tickets. Leadership wants a trustworthy quality number before rolling it out to all tickets. You have 2,000 historical tickets, the human replies that were sent, and outcome data (was the ticket reopened, was the customer satisfied).

choices: A separate, independent grader (a different model and/or human reviewers) blind to which reply is the agent's
Whoever happens to be the fastest available person.
The agent itself, scoring its own confidence level.
The same model that drafted the replies, for consistency.
steps.explain: Separate the doer from the grader, and blind the grader to authorship so it cannot favor the agent. This is the core honesty move.

steps.q: Which metric can you actually measure offline, and how do you get the true outcome?
choices: The average 1-to-5 rating from the drafting model itself.
Blinded independent quality grading offline (agent draft versus the sent human reply, grader blind to authorship), with reopen and resolution outcomes measured later in a controlled pilot or A/B test
Reopen and resolution rate of the agent's drafts on past tickets.
The number of tickets processed per hour by the agent.
steps.explain: The agent's drafts were never sent on the historical tickets, so their reopen and resolution outcomes are counterfactual and cannot be observed offline. Offline you can only compare reply quality, blinded, against the sent human reply; to learn the true outcome you must actually send the agent's replies in a controlled pilot or A/B test.

steps.q: What is the honest baseline to beat?
choices: The best single score seen on any one individual ticket in the whole set.
Zero, since any positive score beats doing nothing.
The human replies that were actually sent on the same tickets
A second copy of the same agent grading the replies.
steps.explain: You must beat the status quo the agent replaces: the human replies, scored on the same items, with an interval around the difference.

steps.q: What goes in the not-measured ledger?
choices: The model's total parameter count and architecture.
The size of the training data set used for the model.
Nothing at all; the evaluation is already complete.
Rare high-stakes tickets (legal, safety, billing disputes) that are underrepresented in the sample and may fail silently
steps.explain: Name what the sample cannot see. Rare, high-cost cases are the classic silent failure; flag them as verification debt for a human checkpoint.

exercise.outcome: You designed an eval with independent grading, an outcome-tied metric, a real baseline, and an explicit ledger of what it cannot measure. That is the difference between a number and a measurement.

outcome: Run the full evaluating-AI discipline on a fresh brief: roles, metric, baseline, claim, and a ledger of the unmeasured.
